In plain English: You should not be able to save a case if the entrustment date is in the future.
What you do: Open New Case Inward. Set Entrustment Date to tomorrow’s date. Try to save.
What you should see: The system should stop you and show a message that the date cannot be in the future.

In plain English: Today’s date for entrustment should be allowed (if everything else is OK).
What you do: Set Entrustment Date to today. Fill other required fields. Save.
What you should see: Save should work unless another rule fails (for example transaction control).

In plain English: If your office rules say you cannot backdate more than X days on entrustment check that rule.
What you do: Ask your admin what “transaction control” says for Entrustment Date (how many days back are allowed). Set the date exactly on the oldest allowed day. Save.
What you should see: Save should work if all other fields are valid.

In plain English: Same rule but one day too old.
What you do: Set Entrustment Date one day earlier than the oldest allowed day. Save.
What you should see: The system should reject and mention the limit (for example cannot be older than X days).

In plain English: You should not save if NPA date is in the future.
What you do: Set NPA Date to tomorrow. Save.
What you should see: Should fail with a message that NPA date cannot be in the future.

In plain English: NPA date can be left blank if your form allows it.
What you do: Leave NPA Date empty if optional. Save with other required fields filled.
What you should see: Should save if everything else is valid.

Section: New Case Inward – List and form show the same date
In plain English: The date on the list screen and the date when you open edit should match.
What you do: Save a case with a known entrustment date (for example 15th of this month). Find it in the list. Open edit.
What you should see: The date in the calendar box should be the same day as in the list (no one-day shift).

In plain English: Some case statuses need money recovered; check that rule.
What you do: Pick a case status that requires recovery but enter zero recovered amount. Save.
What you should see: Should ask for recovery or block save with a clear message.

In plain English: Same as above but with a real recovered amount.
What you do: Pick that status. Add a line with recovered amount greater than zero. Save.
What you should see: Should save if other rules pass.

Section: Who changed what – on each record
In plain English: When you create a new record the system should remember who and when.
What you do: Create any record that has Created / Modified columns. After save open the record again or check the list if those columns show.
What you should see: You should see who created it and the date-time of create; modified should match create on first save.

Section: Who changed what – on each record
In plain English: When you change a record only “last changed” should move.
What you do: Edit an existing record. Change one field. Save. Open again.
What you should see: Created date and creator should stay the same. Modified date-time should be newer.

Section: Audit log (who did what in the system)
In plain English: After you save a business record a line should appear in the audit log.
What you do: Create or update a record. Open the Audit Logs screen (if you have access). Find the latest entry for that action.
What you should see: You should see who did it what action (create/update) and a time that looks like Indian office time not a random foreign time zone.

What you do: Delete a record if your role allows. Check audit log.
What you should see: There should be a delete entry with old data captured.

In plain English: Saving user permissions should stamp dates.
What you do: Save changes on the user permissions matrix (if you use that screen). Open that user’s permission rows or any screen that shows created/modified on those rows.
What you should see: Created and modified times should look sensible in Indian time for when you clicked save.

In plain English: The “Printed on” line on the case details PDF should show today’s date in India.
What you do: Open a case. Print or download Case Details PDF. Read the “Printed on” line.
What you should see: The date should be today’s calendar date in India (dd-mm-yyyy style).

In plain English: Sometimes a saved date looked one day wrong in the past; quick check.
What you do: Pick any saved case. Note the date in the grid. Click edit.
What you should see: The date in the form must be exactly the same day—not one day before or after.

In plain English: Some users cannot edit a case that is already closed or in a final stage (depends on role).
What you do: Log in as the restricted role. Open a case that is already in a final status. Try to edit.
What you should see: Edit may be blocked with a short message (exact text depends on your setup).
